# Transcrição: 05- Course 7 (Lesson 3 - Part 2) - How To Underwrite The Deal

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Now, let's talk about how you're going to underwrite your deal. You need to underwrite the deal and then you need to get a second party to underwrite your underwriting. Remember, it's essential to consider worst case scenarios when investing in a building and when doing your underwriting. You need to be able to determine whether you can handle a period, a year, two years, or even three years where the market's soft. When it comes to underwriting, the same rules apply. You must underwrite for the worst case scenario. Okay, so what is the worst case scenario? When I'm underwriting, I'll take a legal pad and literally start writing out different scenarios. One scenario could be, hey, I paid too much. How do I get out of this property? How does it function if I overpaid for it? By the way, you could overpay for a property. In fact, if you do a lot of real estate deals, there's going to be a time in your life where you do overpay. And let me just say, I have overpaid for deals that were my best deals. My best deals have not been the ones that I got for the lowest price. My deals, my best deals have been those deals where I paid the most of anybody else in the marketplace. You need to do another scenario where their rents never go up. In fact, I'll do that combination where I paid too much, you know, a tad too much, 1%, 2%, too much. And a second scenario where I have no rent growth in year one, year two, year three, year four, year five. I know sooner or later I get rent growth. I'll also use a worst case scenario where the trailing 12, the worst trailing 12 that I can find, remember what trailing 12 is, trailing 12 months of the previous operator. I'll do a worst case scenario underwriting where the 12 months never gets better than the worst 12 months that I can find. I underwrite extremely conservative. The point about underwriting is this, know how you underwrite. Okay? It's like many things in life. You have to know how you do something and then you have to know how someone else does something to value the data, to make the adjustments in the value of the information you're looking at because I consistently underwrite everything extremely conservative. I know when I'm looking at my underwriting that things will probably be a little better in operations than what I looked at on paper. I would rather have the surprise going forward. I also need to know how my underwriting debt arms, my partners, my banks, my lenders, the Fannie Mae's or the people presenting to Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, I need to know how they underwrite. The third party I need to know on the underwriting is how a broker underwrites. Are they extremely aggressive or they conservative or they somewhere in between?

## [03:01 - 06:03]

Some brokers exaggerate everything. Other brokers are very, very conservative in it for the long term and don't want to ruin their reputation by you not being able to hit their numbers. I underwrite extremely conservative. Okay? One of my debt arms, a guy named Bill, he's more conservative than I am. To a default he is conservative. A broker that I work with, he's a little too conservative in deals and sometimes keeps me away from deals I should have bought but he can't see the future. Then there on the other side, there's the guy that every deal is a great deal and every deal is going to bring more rent and every deal can operate at 35% expenses. You have to know the source of the data. Who's giving you the information? Is it CNN? Is it Fox? Is it MTV? Who is it? You understand what I'm saying? Where's the source of data? I always underwrite my deals for the worst case scenario to ensure I can produce positive cash flow. Number one thing I want in a deal is positive cash flow. I'll get appreciation if I can hold the property. Remember that. If I can keep the property, if I picked a great property in a great location that's into a growing market, not a dying market and it has cash flow sooner or later, one day, somehow, one way, big A is going to show up. Appreciation. For example, let me give you a situation. I recently invested in a property and all in, including my rehab fees, I was going to be at $31 million, $31 and a little change. I had the feeling that the rehab, I just had a sense that we could get the rehab done for cheaper and probably save us 400 grand, that we'd land somewhere close to the $30.2 million total closing cost. Now that's significant, but I left my underwriting at $31 million and didn't reduce it by 800 grand. I know this in my calculations. I know I'm over in my calculations. I want to be conservative in my underwriting, but I also need to remember that I'm lying to myself in reverse a little bit and that I need to keep that in mind when I'm making decisions because if you get too conservative on every deal, you'll never buy a deal. I want to make sure that I can withstand a worst case scenario and can still produce cash flow and have a way out to exit and it worked. The return ended up being about 4.5%. I don't care if I'm overpaying. I'm earning 4.5% of my money while I sit and wait. The first three years, I might not have a rent growth. I'll buy everything on that block for that return and you're like, for a 4.5% return, you don't know the location, you don't know the quality of the build, and you don't know how much I love this building. That's eight times, by the way, the 4.5%. That's eight times what any bank pays you in America. Most people today in their savings account are earning 0.12%.

## [06:03 - 09:07]

You might be earning a quarter and some of you are lucky enough to earn a half. You might be in a money market at 1% and I'm earning 4.5 times on a money market that locks your money up. What's the benefits of me owning a 200-unit building in North Miami, integrating location, earning 4.5% or 5% before any rent growth? The reality is this, I could easily be earning 9% or 10% in the years to come. I could go in, spend some money on the units, possibly raise the rents, by the way, we're already doing that. This year, in the second year of ownership, I'll already be closer to a 10% return when I started at 4.5%. See, that's the approach that you need to take, I believe, in your underwriting. Don't look at the best case scenario. Look at the worst. Assume you'll wind up overpaying to get a deal. Again, this is pre-deal. This is before the deal. This is before you went to your financing. This is before your offer was accepted. Assume that you will have to overpay to get the deal. Assume you won't be able to raise your rents. Assume it will never get better than their last worst month. If the numbers work out, assuming all those terrible situations and you can still find cash flow, go for it. If they don't, if the numbers don't work on worst case scenarios, you need to do some more research to find out where the upside here or you need to walk away from this deal. This is not the time for optimism. This is the time to balance between pessimism and optimism. This is the time for assuming the worst, being willing to pull the trigger, and being pleasantly surprised when it's not as bad as you wrote up. Because if you can weather the worst case scenario, this is why I always say, man, I've never lost money in a real estate deal. I have never lost money in an apartment deal. I have never lost money in a property that cash flow positive and that I held on too long enough. I've had two properties that didn't cash flow positive and because I had time on my side, I couldn't lose them, I made money. That's why you should be conservative in your underwriting. But know that when you're being conservative, you still need to be aggressive in the purchase and it's that balance that's going to help you underwrite your deal correctly, pull the trigger, and make money in the future. One more thing on underwriting. From time to time, I'll be adding information as I learn more things. As you ask questions, as you send your questions in, I'll cover those topics and add it to your program. So, here's a deal that I'm currently buying. I'm not going to tell you the name of it, but we're like Monday, today's Friday. Monday I go hard on this deal with, it's a $92 million deal and I'm going to go hard with $920,000, 1% of the purchase price. I know I'm buying this deal. I have my debt lined up. I have three or four, maybe five choices of debt that I'll hear about today. I have a call in a couple hours to go back over the debt.

## [09:07 - 12:11]

I know I'm buying this deal. I know for sure I'm buying this deal because I have run so many worst case scenarios. I'm going to give you an example. He had to do that to present to the seller to get the listing to have the exclusive. Remember, you want to deal with people that have exclusive listings. The package that the broker puts is like a brochure on a car or a house. It's selling the reasons an investor would find the deal appealing. In that, they'll have a pro-pharma. In this pro-pharma, they're saying, okay, you're going to have employees. I'll give you one example of expenses that you need to underwrite. Manager expense. They have $72,000. Manager, $52,000. They were operating the property at 44% expenses, and I have my expenses as high as 53%, including things like taxes. They had taxes at $1,700,000 a year. I figured mine at $1,900,000. ## [12:11 - 12:40]

And we'll find out in the future who's right. By the way, when you're underwriting, you don't need to be right. You need to make money. You need to have all the data so you can pull the trigger so you can make your investors comfortable and so you can know. By the way, you're the number one investor. So you can know when you go to sleep at night, you've underwritten this deal, you haven't taken any shortcuts, you've done your due diligence, you know what you're doing, and worst case is you're going to have two or three years that are down, but not 10 years that are terrible.
